observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
Prepare a correcting entry to recognize the LITS and MTOP under the Computer Software account. Likewise, amortize the same over an asset’s useful economic life to ensure proper valuation of the asset in the financial statements.
Record the purchases of supplies and materials using the perpetual inventory system. Recognize only those purchases paid from the Petty Cash Fund as outright expenses.
Strictly observe proper recording of expenditures in the books of accounts pursuant to the Revised Chart of Accounts as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015, to ensure proper presentation of accounts in the financial statements.

We have recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
Refrain from granting additional cash advances to officials/employees unless the previous cash advance has been fully settled;
Withhold the salaries and other emoluments of personnel with unliquidated cash advances for failure to liquidate the cash advance within the prescribed period pursuant to Paragraphs 9.2 and 9.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002; and
Issue Demand Letters to Accountable Officers and Employees who have not liquidated their cash advances within the prescribed period pursuant to COA Circular No. 97-002 and enforce its immediate liquidation.
For accountable persons who have been transferred or reassigned to a new place of assignment, we have recommended that Management locate their current assignments and send a letter, preferably addressed to the head of office/agency, requesting assistance for the immediate settlement of accounts with the LGU. And for those who are no longer in government service, we have recommended that Management seek a settlement through judicial means.

We have recommended that Management:
Direct the Inventory Committee to conduct periodic physical inventory of supplies every semester. The results of the inventory will be reported in the RPCI and submitted to the Auditor concerned no later than July 31 and January 31 each year for the first and second semesters, respectively.
Require the GSO – Designate and the Municipal Accountant to maintain Stock Cards and SLC, respectively, to ensure proper records of supplies and materials.
Require the GSO-Designate to consolidate the RIS on a weekly basis for which supplies and materials were issued, utilizing the SSMI. The SSMI, along with the original copy of the RIS, shall be submitted to the Chief Accountant, who will compute the cost of supplies issued and the ending inventory using the moving average method. Based on the SSMI, a JEV will be prepared to record the expensed portion of the inventory by employing the appropriate expense account.
Require the GSO – Designate to ensure that the AIR is properly accomplished and indicate in the “Acceptance” portion of the AIR the date of acceptance, marking with a check (✔) whether the delivery is complete or partial in terms of quantity. The inspection officer should indicate in the “Inspection” portion of the AIR the date of inspection and mark with a check (✔) to show that he has inspected, verified, and found the delivered items to be in order regarding quantity and specifications; and
Require the Municipal Accountant to implement the Perpetual Inventory Method when accounting for supplies and materials. This approach involves directing purchased supplies and materials through the appropriate inventory account instead of categorizing them as immediate expenses.

To address the foregoing issues, we have recommended that Management:
Direct the Municipal Accountant and the designated Property/Supply Officer to ensure that all semi-expendable properties recognized under the Office Supplies Expenses amounting to ₱101,515.12 are duly supported with ICS, and properly recorded and reported using the prescribed forms. Adjusting entries are no longer necessary because these were already recognized as expenses, which have already been closed to the Retained Earnings account.
Require the Municipal Accountant to ensure that all semi-expendable properties acquired and issued before CY 2024 are properly adjusted/corrected in the books of accounts in accordance with Annex C of COA Circular 2024-006, which provides illustrative accounting entries to ensure proper recording, adjustments, and reclassification of accounts relative to the increase of the capitalization threshold of PPEs.
Require the Municipal Accountant and the Property/Supply Officer to use the forms, registries, and reports prescribed under Item 4.7 of COA Circular No. 2022-004 dated May 31, 2022, as may be deemed applicable, to ensure that semi-expendable properties acquired and issued in CY 2024 onwards and in the prior years are properly controlled and accounted for.

We have reiterated the prior year's audit recommendation that Management:
Instruct the OIC-Municipal Assessor to trace the historical cost of the identified lots of the LGU acquired through purchase. In cases where a parcel of land was acquired through a non-exchange transaction, such as donations, the prevailing fair value shall be determined at the date of acquisition.
Direct the Municipal Assessor, the Municipal Accountant, and the Municipal Treasurer to reconcile their respective records by considering the TCT/TD Nos.,   area, location, and acquisition cost of the land owned by the LGU.
Advise the Municipal Accountant to prepare appropriate adjusting journal entries after reconciling the aforesaid records for a fair presentation of the Land account in the Financial Statements.
We have recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to prepare and submit a detailed schedule of accounts payable that clearly identifies the nature, supplier, and amount.

Ensure that all supporting documents of disbursement vouchers are complete and duly signed before processing the payment;
Ensure that all financial transaction documents from the Treasury Office are promptly turned over to the Accounting Office for timely recording of transactions. This will contribute to the timely submission of accounts to COA; and
Submit the LGU’s accounts within the first ten days of the following month, in accordance with 9.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-006.
Furthermore, we have recommended that Management provide additional manpower to the Accounting Office by hiring a Job Order primarily to manage the sorting of financial transaction documents, which will facilitate the timely submission of accounts to the COA.

We have recommended that Management:
Require the Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the Municipal Engineer, to immediately submit the lacking documents; and
Direct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that all claims for payment of transactions are thoroughly reviewed for completeness and proper documentation before certifying and endorsing them for payment.

We have recommended that Management:
Instruct the Chairperson of the BAC to ensure that the TWG thoroughly verifies the completeness of the PO during the procurement process; and
Require the Municipal Accountant, as the internal auditor of the LGU, to ensure the completeness of documents when processing the payment for goods, which includes the propriety of the POs.

We have recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant:
To consistently and closely monitor the work of the new staff in the accounting unit. This supervision should be conducted with an emphasis on meeting deadlines to ensure that the required financial reports are submitted within the prescribed period.
Submit Bank Reconciliation Statements timely, that is, within 10 days after the end of each month, to ensure the accuracy and reliability of the cash in bank accounts in the financial statements.
Submit the Trial Balance no later than the twentieth day after the end of the month, in compliance with Section 70, Volume I of the MNGAS for LGUs.

Prepare all the documents required under each procurement stage on time to ensure that all documents are completed at the end of each procurement process.
Submit copies of perfected contracts, approved POs, and supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor within the prescribed period of five (5) working days pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-001 for a timely review and evaluation. Administrative disciplinary action, as provided in Section 4 of the same circular, against the persons responsible, shall be imposed for unjustified failure to comply.

We have recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Budget Officer to:
Monitor the balances of appropriations and obligations regularly, through the Record of Appropriation and Obligation (RAO), to ensure the availability of an appropriation as certified in the CAFOA.
Secure proper authorization of the augmentation of funds prior to its utilization to ensure that all expenditures are paid pursuant to an appropriation ordinance or law.
Refrain from certifying the existence of appropriation for an expenditure without first validating the availability of an appropriation. Otherwise, obligations that exceed appropriations without proper augmentation will be disallowed in audit pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-001.

In light of the above, we have recommended that management appoint a member of the LSB to oversee and track the submission and receipt of the quarterly and annual reports on the SEF budget to the appropriate offices and oversight agencies.

We have recommended that Management:
Direct the GSO-Designate to maintain a record of the history of repair and maintenance of each motor vehicle owned and/or operated by the LGU;
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that payment of repairs and maintenance of each motor vehicle is duly supported with complete documentation; and
Enjoin the Municipal Accountant and the GSO—Designate to ensure that the cost of repairs to the LGU's properties is economical and cost-efficient by determining the current market price of the same or similar equipment before committing to the repairs.

In view of the foregoing, we have recommended that Management:
Require the Planning Officer and Department Heads concerned to submit a comprehensive feasibility study aimed at developing measures to promote maximum utilization of the public market in order to generate more income for the benefit of the Municipality;
Consider the need to increase rates of stall rentals that are responsive and within current industry levels, geared towards making the new market a self-sustaining asset, thus freeing resources used to pay its amortization; and
Instruct the Public Market Administrator – Designate to enforce the collection of the long-outstanding stall rentals in accordance with the lease contracts. Send demand letters as an initial step.

To address the foregoing issues, we have reiterated the prior years’ audit recommendations that Management consider preparing an action plan where different strategies could be applied based on the age and nature of the collectibles and realistically project collections under the circumstances. The following collection strategies may be considered:
Prioritize intensified tax mapping in the first seven barangays identified with the highest delinquent accounts;
Advise the Chief of RPTS to seek further assistance and intervention of barangay officials in the collection of delinquent accounts, and
Instruct the Municipal Treasurer to initiate administrative and judicial actions once all other collection strategies have been thoroughly exhausted and have not yielded results.
Likewise, we have recommended that Management:
Instruct the Municipal Treasurer to reconcile the List of Real Property Tax Delinquency with the records of the Municipal Accountant to ensure that the balances of the RPT and SET receivable are properly reflected in the financial statements.
Require the Municipal Treasurer to furnish the Municipal Accountant with a copy of the Certified List of Taxpayers with an amount due and collectible for the year as a basis to set up the RPT/SET Receivables at the beginning of the yar pursuant to Sections 19 (b) and 20 of the NGAS for LGUs.

observations.recommendation:
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to adjust the CIP and Accounts Payable accounts equivalent to the unbilled amount of ₱633,457.69, based on the RGPPA and SWA as of December 31, 2023;
Direct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that the accrual basis of accounting is adopted in the recording of all ongoing projects as of year-end based on the work accomplishment or percentage of completion as indicated in the RGPPA, regardless of the date of billing or payment; and
Instruct the Municipal Engineer to ensure that ongoing projects reported under RGPPA at year-end are supported by Inspection Reports and SWA as a reliable basis for the Municipal Accountant to recognize the CIP and Accounts Payable accounts at the end of each calendar year.
Management was amenable with the audit observation and signified its willingness to comply with the recommendations of the Audit Team.

Ensure that the derecognition of the cost of the local access road/farm-to-market road from the books of the LGU is duly supported by a MOA showing that the road networks shall be maintained by the barangays concerned;
Ensure that the derecognition of the cost of the Day Care Center is supported by a deed of donation or MOA duly signed by the LGU and the barangay concerned. It is necessary for the donee to accept the responsibility to maintain the Daycare Center; and
Ensure that all infrastructure assets derecognized in the books of the LGU are properly    recorded in the books of the concerned barangays for accountability purposes.

Instruct the OIC-Municipal Assessor to trace the historical cost of the identified lots of the LGU acquired through purchase. In cases where a parcel of land was acquired through a non-exchange transaction, such as donations, the prevailing fair value shall be determined at the date of acquisition;
Direct the Municipal Assessor, the Municipal Accountant, and the Municipal Treasurer to reconcile their respective records by considering the TCT/TD Nos.,   area, location and acquisition cost of the land owned by the LGU; and
Advise the Municipal Accountant to prepare appropriate adjusting journal entries after the reconciliation of the foregoing records for a fair presentation of the Land account in the Financial Statements.
Management agreed to comply with the foregoing audit recommendations.

Require the Municipal Accountant to adopt the accrual method of accounting in the recognition of income;
Require the Municipal Accountant to prepare a journal entry voucher to take up the unrecorded Accounts Receivable and the corresponding income accounts from uncollected stall rentals pursuant to Paragraph 9 of IPSAS 9; and
Require the Municipal Treasurer, in coordination with the Market Supervisor, to provide the Municipal Accountant with the Certified List of Delinquent Stall Holders as the basis for the recognition of accrued income in the books of accounts.

Instruct the Municipal Accountant to maintain a Supplies Ledger Card to record all the receipts and issuances of supplies and materials;
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to set up the appropriate inventory account for all purchases of supplies and materials, except those which are purchased out of the Petty Cash Fund; and
Direct the General Services Officer or the Municipal Treasurer, as the case may be, to maintain stock cards and submit the SSMI together with the original copy of the RIS to the Accounting Unit that will be the basis the preparation of Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) in compliance with the provisions of Sections 114, 120 and 121 of Chapter 7, Volume I of the MNGAS to ensure that the receipt and issuances of various supplies and materials are properly recorded and accounted for.
Management agreed to comply with the recommendations of the Audit Team.

Instruct the Municipal Accountant to trace the details of the “Other Reconciling Items” arising from the reconciliation of SCBAA and Statement of Financial Performance amounting to ₱127,139.45, and include the disclosures in the Notes to the Financial Statements.
Direct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Budget Officer to reconcile records regularly to establish the correctness of the LGU's appropriations, allotments, and obligations.

Ensure that no disbursement voucher shall be paid unless properly documented and signed; and
Ensure that all financial transaction documents from the Treasury Office are promptly forwarded to the Accounting Office for timely recording of transactions. This will contribute to the timely submission of accounts to COA.
Management agreed to comply with the recommendations of the Audit Team.  Also, Management assured the Audit Team to undertake immediate corrective action to rectify the deficiencies highlighted in the audit, and implement measures to ensure timely compliance with COA regulations.

Recommendation:

We have recommended that the ICO-Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Budget Officer refrain from charging prior year’s expenditures to current year appropriations and that proper matching of expenses against appropriation should be observed. Compliance with the above-cited laws, rules and regulations pertaining to charging of prior year’s expenses to current appropriations was strictly recommended in order to avoid disallowance in audit.
observations.reason: The result of post audit of financial transactions disclosed that the local government unit did not charged prior year expenditures to current year appropriations

observations.recommendation:
Recommendations:

We have recommended that the Municipal Treasurer strictly comply with the provisions of existing laws, rules and regulations in the proper handling, custody and disposition of cash to safeguard the government funds from the risk of loss, theft or misuse. For the foregoing deficiencies, we have recommended the following:

The Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Treasurer to deposit intact his collections with the authorized depository bank daily or not later than the next banking day in compliance with the existing laws, rules and regulations to avoid the risk of loss, theft or misuse;

The Collecting Officers remit the collections to the Treasurer on a daily basis; and

The official receipts/accountable forms be issued in strict numerical sequence.
observations.managementAction: Results of cash examination showed that the collecting officers remits their collections to the Municipal Treasurer on a daily basis which were deposited daily and intact to the authorized government depository bank. Also, the official receipts/accountable forms were already issued sequentially.

observations.recommendation:
Recommendation: 

We have recommended that the local government unit strictly adhere with the existing laws, rules and regulations covering the appropriation and utilization of the 20% Local Development Fund to avoid possible sanctions that may be imposed for non-compliance. Further, we have recommended the following:

That the 20% Local Development Fund be optimally utilize in order to deliver the benefits of desirable social, economic and environmental outcomes to the constituents which is essential in the attainment of the constitutional objective of a desired quality of life for all;

That that the Local Chief Executive ensure that the 20% Local Development Fund is utilized within the purposes expressly prescribed by law to ensure that funds for programs/projects/activities geared toward the welfare of its constituents would not be sacrificed; and

To secure approval/ authorization from local Sanggunian for the implementation of projects/programs/activities that were described in generic terms; and

That the local government unit utilize all the unexpended balances of the 20% Local Development Fund for development projects, programs and activities in compliance with the provisions of Section 287 and Section 322 of Republic Act 7160.
observations.reason: Verification of reports disclosed that the local government unit was able to optimally utilized the 20% Local Development Fund for infrastructure projects that were specifically identified and authorized by the Sanggunian Bayan and without the incurrence of improperly charged disbursement against the said fund.

observations.recommendation:
Recommendation:

We have reiterated our previous year recommendation that the local government unit ensure that all of its insurable assets, properties and interests are insured with the General Insurance Fund (GIF) administered by the Government Service Insurance System in compliance with Republic Act No. 656, otherwise known as the “Property Insurance Law”, as amended by Presidential Decree No. 245 dated July 13, 1973 and Administrative Order No. 33 dated August 25, 1987 by identifying all its insurable assets, submitting an inventory report of insurable assets to the System, setting aside in its appropriation the amount needed for premiums and remitting the premiums to the System within the prescribed period to avoid unnecessary losses in the event of damage to, or loss of, assets and properties brought by fortuitous events and guarantee immediate repair or replacement and uninterrupted delivery of public service.
observations.managementAction: The local government unit was able to insure its assets which are significant or vital in the operations.

observations.recommendation:
Recommendation:

We have reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendation that management refrain from paying financial assistance for religious and private purposes in order to avoid suspension or disallowance in audit. The Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer see to it that the provisions of Administrative Order No. 5, series of 2008 of the Department of Social Welfare and Development (DSWD) in the payment of financial assistance under the AICS Program are adhered to and that supporting documents for all financial assistance transactions are complete before effecting payment. The LGU should also formulate a concrete basis/guideline for the selection and retention of student-individual entitled for educational assistance.

However noble the intention of the local government unit in extending financial assistance to the constituents, barangays, schools and various entities in the municipality, strict compliance with the provisions of existing laws, rules and regulations governing payment of financial assistance should be observed.
observations.managementAction: Post audit of accounts and financial transactions revealed that the local government was able to comply in completing the necessary supporting documents before effecting the payment of the financial assistance/donations charged under the AICS.

observations.recommendation:
Recommendation:

observations.managementAction: The local government was able to mark or print with labels indicating that the properties are owned by the government.

observations.recommendation:
Recommendation:

observations.recommendation:
Recommendation:

We have reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendation that the local government unit refrain from allowing deductions from salaries and other benefits accruing to their employees which shall reduce the employee’s monthly net take home pay to an amount lower than Three Thousand Five Hundred Pesos in compliance with Section 47 of Republic Act No. 10924 also known as General Appropriation Act (GAA) for Fiscal Year 2017.
observations.managementAction: Verification of payroll account revealed that some employees were receiving net take home (NTHP) pay that is lesser than the required amount of P4,000.00. However, further verification disclosed that the deductions for the loan amortization were availed during the time when the required net take home pay was less than the current P4,000.00. Nonetheless, the Audit Team noted that the local government unit did not allow other deductions that will reduce the employees NTHP to an amount lower than what was expressly provided in the GAA.

observations.recommendation:
Recommendation:

We have further recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee, who has the responsibility for ensuring that the procuring entity abides by the standards set forth by RA 9184 and its IRR, strictly adhere with all provisions of law, rules and regulations pertaining to procurements to protect at all times the interest of the local government unit.
observations.managementAction: Post audit of accounts and financial transactions disclosed that the local government unit did not leased venue/s for the conduct of seminar/training which contravenes with the aforementioned procurement rules and regulations.

We have further recommended that the other members of the council be encouraged to participate in the preparation of Local Development Plan and Local Investment Plan for Children and other reports in order to have more accurate basis in determining the needs of children and other children related issues that needs immediate attention and action.

We issued Audit Observation Memorandum (AOM) No. 2018 – 007 dated February 2, 2018 to inform management of our audit observations which we have discussed during the exit conference. During the exit conference, management explained that some projects under the 20% Local Development Fund were not implemented due to some unforeseen events that arose during the year. Specifically, management pointed out issues on land ownership as a major factor for the delay in the implementation of some development projects. Though reasonable, the Audit Team emphasized the importance of conducting prior study or survey of the properties involved in order to identify/mitigate possible encumbrances that may affect the timely implementation of development projects. Nonetheless, management had assured the Audit Team of their compliance with the audit recommendations.
We have recommended that the local government unit strictly adhere with the existing laws, rules and regulations covering the appropriation and utilization of the 20% Local Development Fund to avoid possible sanctions that may be imposed for non-compliance. Further, we have recommended the following:
That the 20% Local Development Fund be optimally utilize in order to deliver the benefits of desirable social, economic and environmental outcomes to the constituents which is essential in the attainment of the constitutional objective of a desired quality of life for all;
That that the Local Chief Executive ensure that the 20% Local Development Fund is utilized within the purposes expressly prescribed by law to ensure that funds for programs/projects/activities geared toward the welfare of its constituents would not be sacrificed; and
To secure approval/authorization from local Sanggunian for the implementation of projects/programs/activities that were described in generic terms; and
That the local government unit utilize all the unexpended balances of the 20% Local Development Fund for development projects, programs and activities in compliance with the provisions of Section 287 and Section 322 of Republic Act 7160.

For the foregoing deficiencies, we have recommended the following:
That the Local Chief Executive, as the Head of the Local School Board, adhere strictly with the existing laws, rules and regulations in the prioritization and utilization of Special Education Fund particularly for the operation and maintenance of public schools, construction and repair of school buildings, facilities and equipment, educational research, purchase of books and periodicals and sports development as provided in Sections 100(c) and 272 of the Local Government Code of 1991 and DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 1 s. 2017 in order to ensure an effective educational development within the locality.
That the Local Chief Executive require the concerned school teachers/heads/principals to refund the amount charged against the Special Education Fund to defray registration and transportation expenses in attending seminars/workshops which takes the nature of personal business.
That the Bids and Awards Committee, who has the responsibility for ensuring that the procuring entity abides by the standards set forth by RA 9184 and its IRR, strictly adhere with all provisions of law, rules and regulations pertaining to procurements to protect at all times the interest of the local government unit. Further, we have recommended that the procurement of goods which are similar in nature be consolidated and procured through public bidding and conditions set forth by the law are strictly observed before resorting to alternative modes of procurement.
That management require the concerned officials and employees to submit the required documents. The ICO - Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer ensure that the supporting documents were completely and properly accomplished before affixing their signatures in the disbursement vouchers and before effecting payments pursuant to Section 4 (6) of Presidential Decree No. 1445 to avoid suspension in audit. Compliance with the provisions of the law, rules and regulations pertaining to documentation of claims against government funds was strictly recommended.

We issued Audit Memorandum Observation (AOM) No. 2018-023 dated February 15, 2018 to inform management of our audit observations which we have discussed during the exit conference. During the exit conference, the Audit Team was assured by management their compliance with the audit recommendation
We have reiterated our previous year recommendation that the local government unit ensure that all of its insurable assets, properties and interests are insured with the General Insurance Fund (GIF) administered by the Government Service Insurance System in compliance with Republic Act No. 656, otherwise known as the “Property Insurance Law”, as amended by Presidential Decree No. 245 dated July 13, 1973 and Administrative Order No. 33 dated August 25, 1987 by identifying all its insurable assets, submitting an inventory report of insurable assets to the System, setting aside in its appropriation the amount needed for premiums and remitting the premiums to the System within the prescribed period to avoid unnecessary losses in the event of damage to, or loss of, assets and properties brought by fortuitous events and guarantee immediate repair or replacement and uninterrupted delivery of public service.

We issued Audit Observation Memorandum (AOM) No. 2018 – 019 dated February 15, 2018 to inform management of our audit observations which we have discussed during the exit conference. During the exit conference, the Municipal Planning and Development Officer/Project Coordinator informed the Audit Team that they were exerting efforts in order to collect the proceeds that were extended as a loan to the members of the Association. However, the collectability of the loan/proceeds remained to be difficult since most of the members of the Association could no longer afford to pay the loan. Considering the situation, the Audit Team pointed out the importance of conducting feasibility studies wherein only those livelihood projects that are sustainable will be implemented in the future years to avoid possible loss of scarce government resources. On the other hand, the supporting documents relevant to the Marketing activities of the Yaman Pinoy project were still being collected and organized for submission. Nonetheless, management had assured the Audit Team their compliance with given audit recommendations.
For the foregoing deficiencies, we have reiterated the following previous year’s audit recommendations:
That the Project Coordinator for the implementation of the hollow block making project effect the collection of the proceeds from the sale of the concrete hollow blocks; and
That the concerned officials submit the documents supporting the implementation of the Marketing activity as indicated in the approved project proposal/plan.

We have reiterated our  previous year’s audit recommendation that management refrain from paying financial assistance for religious and private purposes in order to avoid suspension or disallowance in audit. The Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer see to it that the provisions of Administrative Order No. 5, series of 2008 of the Department of Social Welfare and Development (DSWD) in the payment of financial assistance under the AICS Program are adhered to and that supporting documents for all financial assistance transactions are complete before effecting payment. The LGU should also formulate a concrete basis/guidelines for the selection and retention of student-individual entitled for educational assistance.
However noble the intention of the local government unit in extending financial assistance to the constituents, barangays, schools and various entities in the municipality, strict compliance with the provisions of existing laws, rules and regulations governing payment of financial assistance should be observed.

We have reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendations that the concerned LGU officials and employees exert extra effort to intensify collection of delinquent accounts and adopt the procedures and remedies provided in Section 254 and 256 of Republic Act No. 7160, otherwise known as the “Local Government Code of 1991, to enable the collections of unpaid local taxes and employ all necessary measures to enable collection of business taxes from delinquent business establishment owners.  These will provide additional funds to support the projected expenditure of the LGU which may be beneficial to its constituents.
We have recommended that the local government unit to continue to adhere with the existing rules and regulations concerning the withholding and remittance of taxes.

We have recommended that the local government unit continue to adhere with the existing rules and regulations concerning the remittance of GSIS premiums.
We have recommended that the local government unit strictly comply with the existing guidelines on the timely remittance Pag-IBIG Premiums in order to avoid incurrence of surcharges, penalties and interests.
We have recommended that the local government unit continue to adhere with the existing rules and regulations concerning the remittance of PhilHealth premiums.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive, as the chairman of the Local Council for the Protection of Children (LCPC), to optimally utilized the allotted budget and implement more projects/programs/activities as enumerated in DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120 dated July 4, 2012 to address more children related issues in the municipality and fully achieve the objectives of the law creating the fund. Recommendation/s:

We have recommended that the Municipal Treasurer strictly adhere with the guidelines prescribed in the Manual on New Government Accounting System for the preparation of Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms. 

Further, we have recommended that the aforementioned accountable forms issued during the period covered by the cash examination be reflected immediately in the Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms to reflect the correct balance of accountable forms at the end of the month or cut-off date as the case may be. Lastly, we have recommended that the Municipal Treasurer check the statement of accountable forms as to initial balances on hand, receipts, issues and the ending balances on hand every month to ascertain the accuracy of the report.
observations.managementAction: The concerned officials had complied with the audit recommendations.

observations.recommendation:
Recommendation/s:

We have reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendation that the local government unit refrain from allowing deductions from salaries and other benefits accruing to their employees which shall reduce the employee’s monthly net take home pay to an amount lower than Three Thousand Five Hundred Pesos in compliance with Section 52 of Republic Act No. 10717 also known as General Appropriation Act (GAA) for Fiscal Year 2016.
observations.managementAction: The management was amenable with the audit observations. However, they signified their willingness to comply with the given audit recommendations.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Observation No. 19, page 65 of the report. Verification of payroll disclosed that net take home pay was still lower than the amount of Four Thousand Pesos (P4,000.00) as provided in General Appropriation Act (GAA) for Fiscal Year 2017.

observations.recommendation:
Recommendation/s:

We have recommended that the local government unit refrain from acquiring equipment/assets that will remain idle/non-operational. Further, we have recommended that the acquired Plastic Shredder equipment be placed in the location and condition necessary for it to be capable of operating in the manner intended by the management.
observations.managementAction: The management was able to comply with the audit recommendations. The Plastic Shredder equipment had already been placed in the premises of the Material Recovery Facility.

observations.recommendation:
Recommendation/s:

We have reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendations that the concerned LGU officials and employees exert extra effort to intensify collection of delinquent accounts and adopt the procedures and remedies provided in Section 254 and 256 of Republic Act No. 7160, otherwise known as the “Local Government Code of 1991, to enable the collections of unpaid local taxes and employ all necessary measures to enable collection of business taxes from delinquent business establishment owners.  These will provide additional funds to support the projected expenditure of the LGU which may be beneficial to its constituents.
observations.managementAction: Management has informed us that the Treasury and Assessment personnel had conducted tax campaigns during the year in order to intensify their revenue generation and collection. Despite the efforts, the constituents were having difficulties in settling their past due accounts.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Observation No. 21, page 68 of the report. Past due accounts of local taxes and business taxes for fiscal year 2017 amounted P6,286,823.74 and P14,545.20, respectively.

observations.recommendation:
Recommendation/s:

We have recommended that the GAD Plan and Budget and the corresponding Accomplishment Report be submitted to oversight agencies on due date as required under Item 7 – C6 of -DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01 and Item V of  Circular No. 2014 – 001 to ensure the relevance of the GAD PPAs to the identified gender issues and their causes as well as the correctness and alignment of the entries in the GPB Form.
observations.managementAction: The concerned official was not able to submit the  GAD Plan and Budget to the oversight agencies on due date as required under existing laws, rules and regulations.

observations.recommendation:
Recommendation/s:

We have recommended that the unexpended balance of PDAF and DAP totaling P36,450.30 be returned to the Bureau of Treasury so that the funds could be reverted to the unappropriated surplus of the general fund and augment future legitimate funding requirement of the government.
observations.managementAction: Reiterated in Observation No. 21, page 68 of the report. Out of the previous year balance of P36,450.00, the amount of P26,548.20 was remitted to the National Treasury, thus leaving a balance of P9,902.10 for the year ended 2017.
observations.reason: Out of the previous year balance of P36,450.00, the amount of P26,548.20 was remitted to the National Treasury, thus leaving a balance of P9,902.10. |  |  |  | Reiterated in Observation No. 1, page 29 of the report.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the local government unit through the Accounting Unit exhaust all possible means to determine the details and dates of acquisition of the School Buildings recorded under the Property, Plant and Equipment account. After which, compute and record immediately the depreciation in the books of accounts to present fairly the carrying value of the School Buildings account in financial statements.
Below is the suggested pro forma adjusting entry:
Prior Period Adjustment			            P  xxx
Accumulated Depreciation – School Buildings	         P  xxx

We have reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendation that the management strictly adhere with the guidelines and procedures in the recording and reporting of accounts and financial transactions. Further, we have recommended that the Accounting Unit record all capitalizable expenses in accordance with the Philippine Public Sector Accounting Standards (PPSAS) Number 17 and effect necessary adjusting entries to reflect the correct account balances.
Below is the suggested adjusting entry:
Water Supply System					   2,150,000.00
Prior Period Adjustment						2,150,000.00

We have recommended that the Municipal Treasurer in coordination with the Municipal Assessor prepare and furnish the Municipal Accountant a duly certified list of taxpayers showing the names and the amount due and collectible based on RPTAR/Taxpayer’s index card or computer generated report from the system to provide an accurate and reliable basis in the setting-up of receivables for real property taxes and special education taxes at the beginning of the year for fair presentation of the accounts in the financial statements.

We have recommended that the Municipal Treasurer strictly comply with the provisions of existing laws, rules and regulations in the proper handling and custody of cash. Further, we have recommended the following:
That the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Treasurer to deposit intact her collections with the authorized depository bank daily or not later than the next banking day in compliance with the existing laws, rules and regulations to avoid the risk of loss, theft or misuse;
That the Collecting Officers accomplish the Report of Collections and Deposits at the close of each business day and remit the collections to the Treasurer on a daily basis. Further, a cut-off time may be designated by the Municipal Treasurer for which remittance should be made on a daily basis and intact and collections after the cut-off time shall be remitted the following working day; and
That the Collecting Officers exercise due diligence in issuing/using accountable forms in order to avoid/minimize erasures/alterations of material information contained therein. Moreover, the Collecting Officers shall affix his/her signature on the portion wherein erasures/alterations have been made.

We have reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendation that the Bids and Awards Committee, who has the responsibility for ensuring that the procuring entity abides by the standards set forth by RA 9184 and its IRR, strictly adhere with all provisions of law, rules and regulations pertaining to procurements to protect at all times the interest of the local government unit. We have further recommended that:
Pre-bid conference be conducted for contracts to be bid with an approved budget of one million pesos (P1,000,000.00) or more;
Warranty security be required from the contract awardee after the performance of the contract in order to assure that manufacturing defects shall be corrected by the supplier in the case of procurement of goods and that responsibility for any damage or destruction of the works except those occasioned by force majeure in the case of procurement of infrastructure projects be established.
The Bids and Awards Committee who has the responsibility for ensuring that the procuring entity abides by the standards set forth by RA 9184 and its IRR, strictly adhere with all provisions of law, rules and regulations pertaining to procurements to protect at all times the interest of the local government unit. Further, we have recommended that the procurement of goods which are similar in nature be consolidated and procured through public bidding and conditions set forth by the law are strictly observed before resorting to alternative modes of procurement.

We have recommended that the members of the Local School Board, in the preparation of School Board Budget give priority to the programs/projects/activities provided in Sections 100 and 272 of the Local Government Code which govern the disposition of the Special Education Fund. Although noble the intention of the local government unit in extending assistance for various non-priority school activities, we have further recommended that the proceeds of the Special Education Fund be largely utilize for purposes to which the fund was established.

We have reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendation that the concerned personnel exert utmost efforts to meet the demands of timely reporting and submission of accounts.  This is to avoid undue delays that preclude the timely conduct of audit and rendering of the required reports in compliance with existing laws, rules and regulations.

We have reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendation that management require the concerned officials and employees to submit the required documents. The ICO - Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer ensure the completeness of supporting documents before affixing their signatures in the disbursement vouchers and before effecting payments pursuant to Section 4 (6) of Presidential Decree No. 1445 to avoid suspension in audit. Strict ompliance with the provisions of the law, rules and regulations pertaining to documentation of claims against government funds was further recommended.

We have reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendation that claims of Representation and Transportation Allowances be supported with the required documents in order for us to have basis in determining whether the provisions of the aforecited laws, rules and regulations were observed in granting Representation Allowance and Transportation Allowance (RATA). The Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer should see to it that the claims for RATA are supported with complete documentation before certifying and effecting payment.

We have recommended that the local government unit ensure that all of its insurable assets, properties and interests are insured with the General Insurance Fund (GIF) administered by the Government Service Insurance System in compliance with Republic Act No. 656, otherwise known as the “Property Insurance Law”, as amended by Presidential Decree No. 245 dated July 13, 1973 and Administrative Order No. 33 dated August 25, 1987 by identifying all its insurable assets, submitting an inventory report of insurable assets to the System, setting aside in its appropriation the amount needed for premiums and remitting the premiums to the System within the prescribed period to avoid unnecessary losses in the event of damage to, or loss of, assets and properties brought by fortuitous events and guarantee immediate repair or replacement and uninterrupted delivery of public service.

For the foregoing deficiencies, we have reiterated the following previous year’s audit recommendations:
That the Project Coordinator for the implementation of the hollow block making project effect the collection of the proceeds from the sale of the concrete hollow blocks; and
That the concerned officials submit the documents supporting the implementation of the Marketing activity as indicated in the approved project proposal/plan.

We have reiterated our  previous year’s audit recommendation that management refrain from paying financial assistance for religious and private purposes in order to avoid suspension or disallowance in audit. The Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer see to it that the provisions of Administrative Order No. 5, series of 2008 of the Department of Social Welfare and Development (DSWD) in the payment of financial assistance under the AICS Program are adhered to and that supporting documents for all financial assistance transactions are complete before effecting payment. The LGU should also formulate a concrete basis/guidelines for the selection and retention of student-individual entitled for educational assistance.

We have recommended that management refrain from granting allowances to National Government Agency officials which are Personal Services (PS) items in compliance with the aforementioned budget circulars in order to avoid suspension or disallowance in audit.

Considering that full support and cooperation of local government units, being a political subdivision, is an imperious need in order for the national government attain its goals and objectives in modernizing and standardizing government procurement systems, we have recommended that the agency procure its common-use supplies directly from the Procurement Service or its depots in compliance with provisions of Section 21 of GAA 2015 and Sections 1 and 4 of Administrative Order No. 17 dated July 28, 2011.

We have reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendation that local government unit ensure that the Material Recovery Facility (MRF) is efficiently managed and prohibit disposing wastes through incineration in compliance with the provisions of RA 9003 or the Ecological Solid Waste Management Program to protect the constituents against human health and environment hazards.

We have recommended that the Municipal Treasurer strictly adhere with the guidelines prescribed in the Manual on New Government Accounting System for the preparation of Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms. Further, we have recommended that the aforementioned accountable forms issued during the period covered by the cash examination be reflected immediately in the Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms to reflect the correct balance of accountable forms at the end of the month or cut-off date as the case may be. Lastly, we have recommended that the Municipal Treasurer check the statement of accountable forms as to initial balances on hand, receipts, issues and the ending balances on hand every month to ascertain the accuracy of the report.

We have reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendation that the local government unit refrain from allowing deductions from salaries and other benefits accruing to their employees which shall reduce the employee’s monthly net take home pay to an amount lower than Three Thousand Five Hundred Pesos in compliance with Section 52 of Republic Act No. 10717 also known as General Appropriation Act (GAA) for Fiscal Year 2016.

We have recommended that the local government unit refrain from acquiring equipment/assets that will remain idle/non-operational. Further, we have recommended that the acquired Plastic Shredder equipment be placed in the location and condition necessary for it to be capable of operating in the manner intended by the management.

We have reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendations that the concerned LGU officials and employees exert extra effort to intensify collection of delinquent accounts and adopt the procedures and remedies provided in Section 254 and 256 of Republic Act No. 7160, otherwise known as the “Local Government Code of 1991, to enable the collections of unpaid local taxes and employ all necessary measures to enable collection of business taxes from delinquent business establishment owners.  These will provide additional funds to support the projected expenditure of the LGU which may be beneficial to its constituents.
We have recommended that the local government unit to continue to adhere with the existing rules and regulations concerning the withholding and remittance of taxes.
We have recommended that the local government unit continue to adhere with the existing rules and regulations concerning the remittance of GSIS premiums.

We have recommended that the GAD Plan and Budget and the corresponding Accomplishment Report be submitted to oversight agencies on due date as required under Item 7 – C6 of -DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01 and Item V of  Circular No. 2014 – 001 to ensure the relevance of the GAD PPAs to the identified gender issues and their causes as well as the correctness and alignment of the entries in the GPB Form.

We have recommended that the unexpended balance of PDAF and DAP totaling P36,450.30 be returned to the Bureau of Treasury so that the funds could be reverted to the unappropriated surplus of the general fund and augment future legitimate funding requirement of the government.

observations.managementAction: The management explained that they find difficulty in establishing the present status of the previous year’s Property, Plant and Equipment. However, they signified their willingness to comply with the given audit recommendations.

observations.recommendation:
Recommendation/s:

We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant adopt the residual value equivalent to at least five percent (5%) of the cost in the computation of depreciable amount of Property, Plant and Equipment in compliance with the provisions of paragraph 69 of PPSAS 17 and Philippine Application Guidance (PAG) 6 to present fairly the carrying value of the Property, Plant and Equipment accounts in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: Management has adopted the residual value equivalent to  five percent (5%) of the cost in the computation of the depreciable amount.

Recommendation/s:

We have recommended that the management strictly adhere with the guidelines and procedures in the recording and reporting of accounts and financial transactions. For the foregoing deficiencies, we have recommended the following:

The Accounting Unit record all capitalizable expenses in accordance with the Philippine Public Sector Accounting Standards (PPSAS) Number 17 and effect necessary adjusting entries to reflect the correct account balances. 

The Accounting Unit record the purchase of all supplies and materials as inventory following the perpetual inventory method. Issuances thereof should be recorded as they take place using the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI) which shall be submitted by the General Service Officer or Local Treasurer as the case maybe.
observations.managementAction: The concerned officials were amenable to the observations and agreed to comply with the given audit recommendations.

observations.recommendation:
Recommendation/s:

We have reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that management strictly comply with the existing laws, rules and regulations pertaining to granting and liquidation of cash advances. It was further recommended that: 

No additional Cash Advance be given to officials/employees unless the previous cash advances were settled and the liquidating papers were submitted to the Auditor for post-audit and consequently for the issuance of a Credit Notice; 

Certification from the Accountant that previous cash advances have been liquidated and accounted for in the books be required as one of the supporting documents for grant of cash advances;

Withhold the salaries of officials and employees whose cash advances remain unliquidated once efforts yield no encouraging results; and

Appropriate administrative actions against the official/employee be undertaken for the liquidation of their cash advances.

This is without prejudice to the intention of the LGU to install other means of internal control to encourage personnel to adhere with the prescribed policy.
observations.managementAction: The management has agreed to comply with the given audit recommendations.

observations.recommendation:
Recommendation/s:   

In view of the foregoing, we have recommended the following:

That management refrain from paying financial assistance for religious and private purposes in order to avoid suspension or disallowance in audit; 

The Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer see to it that the provisions of Administrative Order No. 5, series of 2008 of the Department of Social Welfare and Development (DSWD) in the payment of financial assistance under the AICS Program are adhered to and that supporting documents for all financial assistance transactions are complete before effecting payment; and 

The LGU formulate a concrete basis/guidelines for the selection and retention of student-individual entitled for educational assistance.

However noble the intention of the local government unit in extending financial assistance to the constituents, barangays, schools and various entities in the municipality, strict compliance with the provisions of existing laws, rules and regulations governing payment of financial assistance should not be set aside.
observations.managementAction: The management has set a certain average grade/rating as criteria in granting educational assistance to student-individual. Nevertheless, the management was amenable with the audit observations and had signified their willingness to comply with the given audit recommendations.
